batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: No finitely additive, isometry-invariant measure can be defined on all subsets of R^3 that assigns positive measure to the unit ball. Equivalently: a solid ball can be decomposed into finitely many pieces and reassembled into two copies of the original ball using rigid motions alone.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Banach & Tarski, 'Sur la décomposition des ensembles de points en parties respectivement congruentes', Fundamenta Mathematicae 6, 1924. Relies on Axiom of Choice.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: The free group F_2 embeds in SO(3) via rotations. The Hausdorff paradox on S^2 decomposes the sphere using non-measurable sets constructed via Axiom of Choice. These pieces are not Lebesgue measurable, so the additive measure property COMPOSE(σ-additivity) cannot extend to COMPLETE(all subsets).
key_references: Banach & Tarski (1924)
Wagon, The Banach-Tarski Paradox, Cambridge UP, 1985
Hausdorff, 'Bemerkung über den Inhalt von Punktmengen', Math. Annalen 75, 1914

batch_5_analysis_approximation.hub_name: Weierstrass Approximation Barrier for Discontinuous Functions
batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: No sequence of polynomials can converge uniformly to a discontinuous function on a compact interval. Polynomial approximation in the sup-norm is impossible outside C[a,b].
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Weierstrass Approximation Theorem (1885) establishes density of polynomials in C[a,b] with sup-norm. The impossibility follows: the uniform closure of polynomials IS C[a,b], so discontinuous functions are unreachable.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: Polynomials are continuous, and uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (a topological closure property). The uniform closure of the polynomial algebra on [a,b] equals C[a,b] exactly. Discontinuous functions lie outside this closure.
key_references: Weierstrass (1885)
Stone, 'The Generalized Weierstrass Approximation Theorem', Math. Magazine, 1948
Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch. 7

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: There exists a continuous function on [0,2π] whose Fourier series diverges at a point. Pointwise convergence of Fourier series is not guaranteed even for continuous functions.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: du Bois-Reymond, 'Untersuchungen über die Convergenz und Divergenz der Fourierschen Darstellungsformeln', Abh. Akad. München 12, 1876. Strengthened by Carleson (1966) to convergence a.e. for L^2.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: The Dirichlet kernel D_n has unbounded L^1 norm (grows as log n). By the Banach-Steinhaus (uniform boundedness) theorem, the set of continuous functions where S_n(f,x) diverges is residual (comeager) in C[0,2π]. The partial sum operator sequence is not uniformly bounded in the sup-norm.
key_references: du Bois-Reymond (1876)
Carleson, 'On convergence and growth of partial sums of Fourier series', Acta Math. 116, 1966
Katznelson, An Introduction to Harmonic Analysis, 3rd ed., Ch. 2

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: No approximation scheme can achieve a universal rate of convergence for all continuous functions simultaneously. For any sequence of approximation operators L_n with dim(L_n)=n, there exist continuous functions that converge arbitrarily slowly.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Follows from the Bernstein lethargy theorem (1938). For neural networks: Barron (1993) showed O(1/√n) for functions with bounded Fourier moment, but DeVore et al. (1989) proved curse-of-dimensionality lower bounds for general smooth functions.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: By the Bernstein lethargy theorem, for any decreasing sequence ε_n→0, there exists f in the target space such that the best approximation error E_n(f) decreases no faster than ε_n. The unit ball in C[a,b] is not compact, so no uniform rate covers all elements.
key_references: Bernstein, 'On the inverse problem of best approximation', 1938
Barron, 'Universal approximation bounds for superpositions of a sigmoidal function', IEEE Trans IT, 1993
DeVore, Howard & Micchelli, 'Optimal nonlinear approximation', Manuscripta Math. 63, 1989

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: Kolmogorov's Superposition Theorem (1957) represents any continuous multivariate function as compositions and sums of univariate functions, BUT the inner functions are highly non-smooth (typically nowhere-differentiable), making the representation computationally intractable for smooth approximation.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Kolmogorov, 'On the representation of continuous functions of many variables by superposition of continuous functions of one variable and addition', Doklady 114, 1957. Computational intractability: Vitushkin (1964), Fridman (1967).
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: The inner functions φ_q are universal (independent of f) but must encode the topology of R^n into R, requiring space-filling-curve-like behavior. This forces them to be at best Hölder continuous with exponent depending on dimension but never Lipschitz. Smoothness and universality are mutually exclusive.
key_references: Kolmogorov (1957)
Arnold, 'On functions of three variables', Doklady 114, 1957
Vitushkin, 'On representation of functions by means of superpositions and related topics', L'Enseignement Math. 23, 1977

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: For functions in the Sobolev space W^s_p([0,1]^d), the optimal approximation rate using n parameters is O(n^{-s/d}). As dimension d grows, exponentially many parameters are needed to maintain a fixed accuracy. No method escapes this for the full Sobolev class.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Novak & Woźniakowski, 'Tractability of Multivariate Problems', EMS 2008-2012 (3 volumes). Lower bounds: Bakhvalov (1959), Smolyak (1963). The term coined by Bellman (1957).
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: The ε-covering number of the unit ball in W^s_p([0,1]^d) grows as exp(c·(1/ε)^{d/s}). Any linear or nonlinear n-term approximation scheme must respect this metric entropy lower bound (Kolmogorov n-widths). The volume of the unit cube grows exponentially with d while the number of sample points grows polynomially.
key_references: Bellman, Dynamic Programming, Princeton UP, 1957
Novak & Woźniakowski, Tractability of Multivariate Problems, EMS, 2008
Bakhvalov, 'On the approximate calculation of multiple integrals', Vestnik MGU, 1959

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: For any unbiased estimator θ̂ of a parameter θ, the variance satisfies Var(θ̂) ≥ 1/I(θ), where I(θ) is the Fisher information. No unbiased estimator can have variance below this bound.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Cramér, Mathematical Methods of Statistics, Princeton UP, 1946. Rao, 'Information and accuracy attainable in the estimation of statistical parameters', Bull. Calcutta Math. Soc. 37, 1945.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality applied to the score function and the estimator yields the bound. The Fisher information I(θ) = E[(∂log f/∂θ)²] is the curvature of the KL divergence at θ, setting a fundamental precision floor. Equality holds iff the estimator is an affine function of the score.
key_references: Cramér (1946)
van Trees, Detection, Estimation, and Modulation Theory, Wiley, 1968
Lehmann & Casella, Theory of Point Estimation, 2nd ed., Springer, 1998

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: Polynomial interpolation on equispaced nodes diverges for certain analytic functions as the degree increases. Uniform convergence of interpolation polynomials is impossible on equispaced grids for functions with poles near the real axis.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Runge, 'Über empirische Funktionen und die Interpolation zwischen äquidistanten Ordinaten', Zeitschrift für Mathematik und Physik 46, 1901.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: The Lebesgue constant Λ_n for equispaced nodes grows as 2^n/(e·n·log n). For Runge's function f(x)=1/(1+25x²), the interpolation error is bounded below by the Lebesgue constant times the best approximation error. The poles at ±i/5 lie within the Bernstein ellipse for large n, causing the interpolant to diverge.
key_references: Runge (1901)
Trefethen, Approximation Theory and Approximation Practice, SIAM, 2013
Brutman, 'Lebesgue functions for polynomial interpolation—a survey', Annals Num. Math. 4, 1997

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: The span of monomials {x^{λ_n}} is dense in C[0,1] if and only if Σ 1/λ_n = ∞. If the exponents are too sparse (series converges), the monomial system cannot approximate all continuous functions.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Müntz, 'Über den Approximationssatz von Weierstrass', Schwarz Festschrift, 1914. Szász, 'Über die Approximation stetiger Funktionen durch lineare Aggregate von Potenzen', Math. Annalen 77, 1916.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: When Σ 1/λ_n < ∞, the closed span of {x^{λ_n}} in C[0,1] is a proper subspace. The dual characterization shows there exist nonzero measures orthogonal to all x^{λ_n}. The missing monomials create gaps in the frequency coverage that cannot be compensated by the remaining terms.
key_references: Müntz (1914)
Szász (1916)
Borwein & Erdélyi, Polynomials and Polynomial Inequalities, Springer, 1995

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: For any triangular array of interpolation nodes in [a,b], there exists a continuous function for which the interpolating polynomial sequence diverges. No single choice of nodes guarantees convergence for ALL continuous functions.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Faber, 'Über die interpolatorische Darstellung stetiger Funktionen', Jahresbericht DMV 23, 1914. Related: Bernstein (1916) on the divergence phenomenon.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: The Banach-Steinhaus theorem applies: the interpolation operators L_n: C[a,b]→C[a,b] are bounded linear. The Lebesgue constant Λ_n = ||L_n|| grows without bound for ANY node array (Λ_n ≥ (2/π)log(n+1) - C). By uniform boundedness, pointwise divergence occurs on a residual set.
key_references: Faber (1914)
Erdős & Vértesi, 'On the Lebesgue function of interpolation', Functional Analysis and Approximation, 1981
Rivlin, An Introduction to the Approximation of Functions, Dover, 1981

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: For any sequence of nested finite-dimensional subspaces V_1 ⊂ V_2 ⊂ ... in a Banach space X, and any rate ε_n → 0, there exists an element f ∈ X whose best approximation error E_n(f) = inf_{g ∈ V_n} ||f-g|| decreases no faster than ε_n.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Bernstein, 'On the inverse problem of the theory of best approximation of continuous functions', Sochineniya II, 1938. Tyuriemskih extended to general Banach spaces, 1967.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: The quotient space X/V_n has infinite dimension for each n. The best approximation map is a projection onto V_n, and the sequence of distances d(f, V_n) can be arranged to match any prescribed rate by constructing f as a series with carefully chosen coefficients in directions nearly orthogonal to each V_n.
key_references: Bernstein (1938)
Tyuriemskih, 'On a generalization of a theorem of Bernstein', 1967
Timan, Theory of Approximation of Functions of a Real Variable, Pergamon, 1963

batch_5_analysis_approximation.impossibility_statement: A bandlimited signal with bandwidth B cannot be perfectly reconstructed from samples taken at rate less than 2B. Sub-Nyquist sampling irreversibly loses information.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.formal_source: Shannon, 'Communication in the Presence of Noise', Proc. IRE 37, 1949. Nyquist, 'Certain topics in telegraph transmission theory', Trans. AIEE 47, 1928.
batch_5_analysis_approximation.why_closure_fails: In frequency domain, sampling at rate f_s causes spectral copies at multiples of f_s. When f_s < 2B, adjacent copies overlap (aliasing), making the original spectrum unrecoverable. The map from continuous to sampled signal is not injective below the Nyquist rate.
key_references: Shannon (1949)
Nyquist (1928)
Unser, 'Sampling—50 years after Shannon', Proc. IEEE, 2000

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: If markets are perfectly informationally efficient (prices fully reflect all information), then no agent has incentive to acquire costly information. But if no one acquires information, prices cannot reflect it. Perfect informational efficiency and costly information acquisition are mutually incompatible.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Grossman & Stiglitz, 'On the Impossibility of Informationally Efficient Markets', American Economic Review 70(3), 1980.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: In equilibrium, informed traders' profits must cover information costs. If prices reveal all information, informed and uninformed traders earn equal returns, so information acquisition has negative expected utility. The fixed-point equation for equilibrium price informativeness has no solution at full revelation when costs are positive.
key_references: Grossman & Stiglitz (1980)
Kyle, 'Continuous Auctions and Insider Trading', Econometrica 53, 1985
Stiglitz, 'Information and the Change in the Paradigm in Economics', AER 92, 2002

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: A banking system cannot simultaneously provide liquidity transformation (short-term deposits funding long-term loans), maintain sequential service (first-come-first-served withdrawal), and be immune to self-fulfilling bank runs, without deposit insurance or a lender of last resort.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Diamond & Dybvig, 'Bank Runs, Deposit Insurance, and Liquidity', Journal of Political Economy 91(3), 1983.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: The demand-deposit contract creates two Nash equilibria: one where agents withdraw only for genuine liquidity needs (efficient), and one where all agents rush to withdraw (bank run). The sequential service constraint means early withdrawers are paid in full while late withdrawers get nothing if assets are illiquid. This strategic complementarity in withdrawal decisions makes the run equilibrium self-fulfilling.
key_references: Diamond & Dybvig (1983)
Allen & Gale, 'Financial Contagion', JPE 108, 2000
Goldstein & Pauzner, 'Demand-Deposit Contracts and the Probability of Bank Runs', JF 60, 2005

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: Efficient bargaining over externalities (Coase's result) fails when any of: (1) transaction costs are positive, (2) property rights are unclear, (3) parties are asymmetrically informed, or (4) there are many affected parties (public goods/commons). These conditions are generic, making the Coase result the exception.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Coase, 'The Problem of Social Cost', Journal of Law and Economics 3, 1960. Impossibility conditions formalized by: Myerson & Satterthwaite (1983) for bilateral asymmetric information; Mailath & Postlewaite (1990) for large-number inefficiency.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: Under asymmetric information, the Myerson-Satterthwaite theorem shows no mechanism is simultaneously individually rational, incentive compatible, budget balanced, and ex-post efficient. Transaction costs create a wedge between willingness-to-pay and willingness-to-accept. With many parties, free-riding prevents efficient Coasean bargaining.
key_references: Coase (1960)
Myerson & Satterthwaite (1983)
Mailath & Postlewaite, 'Asymmetric Information Bargaining Problems with Many Agents', RES 57, 1990

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: Econometric models estimated under one policy regime cannot be used to predict outcomes under a different policy regime, because agents' decision rules (and hence the model parameters) change endogenously with policy. Policy-invariant reduced-form models are impossible when agents are forward-looking.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Lucas, 'Econometric Policy Evaluation: A Critique', Carnegie-Rochester Conference Series on Public Policy 1, 1976.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: Reduced-form parameters are functions of both structural (preference/technology) parameters AND policy rule parameters. When policy changes, rational agents re-optimize, shifting the mapping from state variables to outcomes. The reduced form is not invariant under the policy intervention it is used to evaluate, violating the autonomy requirement of Haavelmo (1944).
key_references: Lucas (1976)
Sargent, 'The Ends of Four Big Inflations', in Inflation, 1982
Haavelmo, 'The Probability Approach in Econometrics', Econometrica 12, 1944

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: There is no permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment. In the long run, monetary policy can only choose the inflation rate, not the unemployment rate. Attempts to hold unemployment below the natural rate permanently lead to accelerating inflation.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Friedman, 'The Role of Monetary Policy', AER 58, 1968. Phelps, 'Phillips Curves, Expectations of Inflation and Optimal Unemployment over Time', Economica 34, 1967.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: Adaptive (Friedman) or rational (Lucas/Sargent) expectations adjust to actual inflation. When expected inflation equals actual inflation, the Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate u*. Any attempt to maintain u < u* requires continuously accelerating inflation to stay ahead of expectations, which is unsustainable.
key_references: Friedman (1968)
Phelps (1967)
Lucas, 'Expectations and the Neutrality of Money', JET 4, 1972

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: The Condorcet Jury Theorem (majority voting aggregates information optimally) fails when: (1) voters are correlated (common information sources), (2) voters vote strategically rather than sincerely, or (3) voter competence is below 0.5. Under strategic voting, sincere revelation is not a Nash equilibrium in general.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Condorcet, 'Essai sur l'application de l'analyse à la probabilité des décisions rendues à la pluralité des voix', 1785. Strategic failure: Austen-Smith & Banks, 'Information Aggregation, Rationality, and the Condorcet Jury Theorem', APSR 90, 1996.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: Under strategic voting, a pivotal voter conditions on being pivotal, which changes the information inference. With correlated signals, the effective number of independent information sources grows sub-linearly. When competence p < 0.5, majority voting amplifies errors (anti-jury theorem). These three failures make the original theorem's conditions non-generic.
key_references: Condorcet (1785)
Austen-Smith & Banks (1996)
Ladha, 'Condorcet's jury theorem in light of de Finetti's theorem', Social Choice and Welfare 10, 1993

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: The Second Welfare Theorem (any Pareto efficient allocation can be decentralized as a competitive equilibrium with appropriate lump-sum transfers) fails when: (1) preferences are non-convex, (2) lump-sum transfers are informationally infeasible, (3) increasing returns to scale exist. Redistribution without efficiency loss is generically impossible.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Arrow, 'An Extension of the Basic Theorems of Classical Welfare Economics', 1951. Impossibility of lump-sum transfers: Mirrlees, 'An Exploration in the Theory of Optimum Income Taxation', RES 38, 1971. Non-convexity: Guesnerie, 'Pareto Optimality in Non-Convex Economies', Econometrica 43, 1975.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: Lump-sum transfers require knowledge of agents' types (abilities, preferences), which are private information. By the revelation principle, any incentive-compatible mechanism must impose distortions. Non-convex preferences break the supporting hyperplane theorem, so price-based decentralization of efficient allocations becomes impossible. Increasing returns create natural monopoly, violating the price-taking assumption.
key_references: Arrow (1951)
Mirrlees (1971)
Guesnerie (1975)
Stiglitz, 'Pareto Efficient and Optimal Taxation and the New New Welfare Economics', Handbook of Public Economics, 1987

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: Free trade cannot benefit all factors of production simultaneously. In a Heckscher-Ohlin framework, trade liberalization necessarily raises the real return to the abundant factor and lowers the real return to the scarce factor. Pareto-improving trade is impossible without compensation.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Stolper & Samuelson, 'Protection and Real Wages', Review of Economic Studies 9, 1941. Factor price equalization: Samuelson, 'International Trade and the Equalisation of Factor Prices', Economic Journal 58, 1948.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: With two goods, two factors, and constant returns to scale, goods prices uniquely determine factor prices (by the zero-profit conditions and non-reversibility of factor intensities). A change in relative goods prices (from trade) must raise one factor price and lower the other via the magnification effect: factor price changes are amplified relative to goods price changes.
key_references: Stolper & Samuelson (1941)
Samuelson (1948)
Deardorff, 'The general validity of the Heckscher-Ohlin theorem', AER 72, 1982

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: Any voting system with at least 3 alternatives that is surjective (every alternative can win) and strategy-proof (no voter benefits from misreporting preferences) must be dictatorial. Non-dictatorial, strategy-proof social choice functions do not exist for ≥3 alternatives.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Gibbard, 'Manipulation of Voting Schemes: A General Result', Econometrica 41, 1973. Satterthwaite, 'Strategy-proofness and Arrow's conditions', JET 10, 1975.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: Strategy-proofness requires monotonicity and a strong form of independence. With ≥3 alternatives, any SCF satisfying these conditions must be dictatorial by an argument paralleling Arrow's theorem. The proof proceeds by showing pivotal voters must be decisive, and decisiveness propagates to dictatorship.
key_references: Gibbard (1973)
Satterthwaite (1975)
Barberà, 'Strategy-proof Social Choice', Handbook of Social Choice and Welfare, 2011

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: No social decision function can simultaneously satisfy: (1) unrestricted domain, (2) Pareto optimality, and (3) minimal liberalism (each individual is decisive over at least one pair of alternatives in their personal sphere). Individual rights and Pareto efficiency are incompatible.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Sen, 'The Impossibility of a Paretian Liberal', JPE 78, 1970.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: When individuals have meddlesome preferences (preferences over what others do in their private sphere), the liberal rights assignment can produce a cycle with the Pareto principle. Individual A's right over {x,y} and B's right over {z,w} can conflict with unanimous preference, creating no consistent social choice. The Pareto principle and rights-based constraints generate contradictory orderings.
key_references: Sen, 'Liberty, Unanimity and Rights', Economica 43, 1976
Gaertner, Pattanaik & Suzumura, 'Individual Rights Revisited', Economica 59, 1992

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: Purchasing Power Parity (equal price levels across countries) is impossible when countries differ in relative productivity between tradable and non-tradable sectors. Higher-productivity countries systematically have higher price levels for non-tradables.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Balassa, 'The Purchasing-Power Parity Doctrine: A Reappraisal', JPE 72, 1964. Samuelson, 'Theoretical Notes on Trade Problems', RES 46, 1964.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: Trade equalizes tradable goods prices. Internal labor mobility equalizes wages across sectors within a country. If country A has higher tradable-sector productivity, its wages are higher. Its non-tradable prices (set by wages, not trade) are also higher. The composition of the price index differs from the composition of traded goods, so aggregate price levels diverge systematically with productivity.
key_references: Balassa (1964)
Samuelson (1964)
Rogoff, 'The Purchasing Power Parity Puzzle', JEL 34, 1996

batch_8_economics_social_science.impossibility_statement: Ordinal utility theory provides no basis for interpersonal comparisons of utility. Without cardinal, comparable utility functions, social welfare functions requiring utility aggregation (utilitarian, Rawlsian, etc.) cannot be constructed from individual choice data alone.
batch_8_economics_social_science.formal_source: Robbins, 'Interpersonal Comparisons of Utility: A Comment', Economic Journal 48, 1938. Formalized: Sen, 'Interpersonal Aggregation and Partial Comparability', Econometrica 38, 1970.
batch_8_economics_social_science.why_closure_fails: Ordinal utility is invariant under arbitrary monotone transformations. If u_A and u_B are utility functions for agents A and B, any monotone transforms f(u_A) and g(u_B) are equally valid. The statement 'A gains more than B loses' has no meaning under ordinal transformations, because the gain/loss magnitudes are artifacts of the representation.
key_references: Robbins (1938)
Hammond, 'Equity, Arrow's Conditions, and Rawls' Difference Principle', Econometrica 44, 1976
